en.extract:
Grauer's cuckooshrike (Ceblepyris graueri) is a little known species of bird in the family Campephagidae.
It is endemic to the Democratic Republic of the Congo. It is found in mid-elevation montane forests, where it can be locally common. Similar to other species endemic to the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, it is rarely reported due to ongoing armed conflict in the Albertine Rift.


Description


Appearance
A slender, medium sized passerine with a relatively long tail and graceful appearance. It's white belly is contrasted by a gray head and gray upperparts, which are a darker slaty gray in the male and a paler gray in the female. It is approximately 22 centimetres (8.7 in) in length.


Vocalizations
Unknown.


Taxonomy
Grauer's cuckooshrike is part of an African radiation of cuckooshrikes including gray cuckooshrike Ceblepyris caesius, madagascar cuckooshrike C. cinereus, Comoro cuckooshrike C. cucullatus, and white-breasted cuckooshrike C. pectoralis. Grauer's cuckooshrike forms the outgroup to all other mainland African cuckooshrikes.


Etymology
The name commemorates the German zoologist  Rudolf Grauer who collected natural history specimens in the Belgian Congo.


Regional Variation
Monotypic.


Distribution and habitat


Distribution
Grauer's cuckooshrike is endemic to the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo. Reports from the Rwenzoris of Uganda are unsubstantiated and presently considered unconfirmed.
Due to the political instability of the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, it is hard to assess the current status and distribution of birds in the region. However, a team from the Wildlife Conservation Society reported in 2013 that Grauer's Cuckoshrike "was particularly abundant" in the Kisimba-Ikobo Community Reserve, but they were "attacked by people antagonistic to the reserve". Grauer's cuckooshrike is known from other protected areas, such as Kahuzi-Biega National Park, but there are few (if any) recent records from these regions.


Habitat
This species is known from montane forest and other high elevation forested habitats in the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo. Of the 51 specimens included in a recent study, all were from only 5 mountain ranges in the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, with an altitudinal range of 1140-1900 m 1,140–1,900 metres (3,740–6,230 ft). The species is generally found at intermediate elevations between the lower-elevation blue cuckooshrike Cyanograucalus azureus and the higher-elevation gray cuckooshrike.


Behavior


Foraging and General Behavior
Not much is known of the behavior of this species, and it is presumably similar to its congeners.


Reproduction
Lays eggs at the end of the rainy seasons, with reports from January, May, and June. Not much is known regarding the breeding behavior of this species, and more research is required.


Conservation
This species is considered Least Concern. More studies and surveys are required to assess its current status, but these are impractical given security concerns within much of this species' range. It is potentially threatened by climate change, and it has not been recently reported from some areas where it was previously known. Other threats include deforestation.


References

'''Grauer's cuckooshrike''' ('''''Ceblepyris graueri''''') is a little known species of [[bird]] in the family [[Campephagidae]].
It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]]. It is found in mid-elevation [[Albertine Rift montane forests|montane forests]], where it can be locally common. Similar to other species endemic to the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, it is rarely reported due to ongoing [[Kivu conflict|armed conflict]] in the [[Albertine Rift]].

==Description==
===Appearance===
A slender, medium sized passerine with a relatively long tail and graceful appearance. It's white belly is contrasted by a gray head and gray upperparts, which are a darker slaty gray in the male and a paler gray in the female. It is approximately {{convert|22|cm|in}} in length.<ref name="bow" />

===Vocalizations===
Unknown.<ref name="bow" />

==Taxonomy==
Grauer's cuckooshrike is part of an African radiation of cuckooshrikes including [[gray cuckooshrike]] ''Ceblepyris caesius'', [[madagascar cuckooshrike]] ''C. cinereus'', [[Comoro cuckooshrike]] ''C. cucullatus'', and [[white-breasted cuckooshrike]] ''C. pectoralis''.<ref name="biogeo_cuckooshrikes">{{cite journal |last1=Jønsson |first1=Knud A. |last2=Bowie |first2=Rauri C. K. |last3=Nylander |first3=Johan A. A. |last4=Christidis |first4=Les |last5=Norman |first5=Janette A. |last6=Fjeldså |first6=Jon |title=Biogeographical history of cuckoo-shrikes (Aves: Passeriformes): transoceanic colonization of Africa from Australo-Papua |journal=Journal of Biogeography |date=2010 |volume=37 |issue=9 |pages=1767–1781 |doi=10.1111/j.1365-2699.2010.02328.x|s2cid=52105369 }}</ref> Grauer's cuckooshrike forms the [[Outgroup (cladistics)|outgroup]] to all other mainland African cuckooshrikes.<ref name="biogeo_cuckooshrikes" />

===Etymology===
The name commemorates the German zoologist  [[Rudolf Grauer]] who collected natural history specimens in the Belgian Congo.<ref>{{cite book|last=Beolens|first=Bo|title=Whose Bird? Men and Women Commemorated in the Common Names of Birds|year=2003|publisher=Christopher Helm|location=London|page=94|author2=Watkins, Michael}}</ref>

===Regional Variation===
Monotypic.

==Distribution and habitat==
===Distribution===
Grauer's cuckooshrike is endemic to the eastern [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]]. Reports from the [[Rwenzoris]] of Uganda are unsubstantiated and presently considered unconfirmed.<ref name="mann_76">{{cite journal |last1=Mann |first1=Clive F. |title=Some recent changes in our knowledge of bird distribution in East Africa |journal=Journal of East African Natural History |date=1976 |issue=157 |pages=1–24}}</ref><ref name="scopus_badrec">{{cite journal |last1=Fisher |first1=David |last2=Pearson |first2=David |last3=Hunter |first3=Nigel |title=East African Records Committee (EARC) Special Report Species included for East African countries in Britton (1980) which have since been considered unacceptable |journal=Scopus |date=2016 |volume=36 |issue=2 |pages=55–56}}</ref>

Due to the political instability of the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, it is hard to assess the current status and distribution of birds in the region. However, a team from the [[Wildlife Conservation Society]] reported in 2013 that Grauer's Cuckoshrike "was particularly abundant" in the Kisimba-Ikobo Community Reserve, but they were "attacked by people antagonistic to the reserve".<ref name="wcs_2015">{{cite journal |last1=Plumptre |first1=A. J. |last2=Ayebare |first2=S. |last3=Kujirakwinja |first3=D. |title=Priority areas for conservation in the Maiko, Tayna, Kahizi Biega landscape |journal=Unpublished Report for USAID and USFWS |date=2015}}</ref> Grauer's cuckooshrike is known from other protected areas, such as [[Kahuzi-Biega National Park]], but there are few (if any) recent records from these regions.<ref name="wcs_2015" />

===Habitat===
This species is known from [[Albertine Rift montane forests|montane forest]] and other high elevation forested habitats in the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo.<ref name="drc_iba">{{cite journal |last1=Demey |first1=Ron |last2=Louette |first2=Michel |title=Democratic Republic of Congo |journal=Important Bird Areas in Africa and Associated Islands: Priority Sites for Conservation |date=2001 |pages=199–218}}</ref><ref name="rwenzori_climate">{{cite journal |last1=Eilu |first1=G. |last2=Galabuzi |first2=C. |last3=Waiswa |first3=D. |last4=Oriekot |first4=J. |last5=Kakuru |first5=W. |last6=Mwavu |first6=E. N. |last7=Orikiriza |first7=L. B. |last8=Turyahabwe |first8=N. |last9=Shofuna |first9=A. |last10=Kasangaki |first10=A. |last11=Tumwebaze |first11=S. B. |title=Impact of climate change on the species of restricted range in Rwenzori Mountains National Park |journal=Final Report to the Uganda Wildlife Authority from Makerere University |date=2013}}</ref> Of the 51 specimens included in a recent study, all were from only 5 mountain ranges in the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, with an altitudinal range of 1140-1900 m {{convert|1140|-|1900|m|ft}}.<ref name="rift_alt">{{cite journal |last1=Bober |first1=S. O. |last2=Herremans |first2=M. |last3=Louette |first3=Michel |last4=Kerbis Peterhans |first4=Julian C. |last5=Bates |first5=John M. |title=Geographical and altitudinal distribution of birds endemic to the Albertine Rift |journal=Ostrich Supplement |date=2001 |volume=15 |pages=189–196}}</ref> The species is generally found at intermediate elevations between the lower-elevation [[blue cuckooshrike]] ''Cyanograucalus azureus'' and the higher-elevation [[gray cuckooshrike]].<ref name="bow">{{cite journal |last1=Taylor |first1=Barry |editor-first1=Josep |editor-first2=Andrew |editor-first3=Jordi |editor-first4=David |editor-first5=Eduardo |editor-last1=Del Hoyo |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-last4=Christie |editor-last5=De Juana |title=Grauer's Cuckooshrike (''Coracina graueri'') |journal=Birds of the World |date=4 March 2020 |doi=10.2173/bow.gracus1.01|s2cid=216251441 }}</ref>

==Behavior==
===Foraging and General Behavior===
Not much is known of the behavior of this species, and it is presumably similar to its congeners.

===Reproduction===
Lays eggs at the end of the rainy seasons, with reports from January, May, and June.<ref name="bow" /> Not much is known regarding the breeding behavior of this species, and more research is required.

==Conservation==
This species is considered Least Concern. More studies and surveys are required to assess its current status, but these are impractical given security concerns within much of this species' range. It is potentially threatened by climate change,<ref name="rwenzori_climate" /> and it has not been recently reported from some areas where it was previously known.<ref name="wcs_2015" /> Other threats include deforestation.<ref name="bow" />

zh.extract:
格氏鹃鵙（学名：Coracina graueri）是山椒鸟科鸦鹃鵙属的一种。分布于刚果民主共和国和乌干达。其自然栖息地为亚热带或热带的湿润山地。该物种受栖息地破坏威胁。


参考文献
BirdLife International. Coracina graueri. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2009.   [2010-10-20] （英语）.
